---
title: "Detailed Syllabus and Recommended Pacing"
---

## Course Modes

This curriculum supports three teaching modes:

- a 14-week semester course;
- a 28-week self-study sequence;
- a modular graduate seminar or reading course.

The chapter dependencies are strong enough that the intended order matters. Probability and likelihood precede priors and posteriors; priors and posteriors precede computational methods; computational methods precede realistic modeling and diagnostics.

## Semester Syllabus

| Week | Topics | Core mathematical focus | Computational focus | Suggested deliverable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Probability spaces, random variables, conditioning | Conditional expectation, tower property | Simulation in `R` | Short derivation set |
| 2 | Likelihood theory and asymptotics | Score, Fisher information, MLE | Likelihood surfaces in `R` | Problem set 1 |
| 3 | Bayes' theorem, exchangeability, updating | Posterior factorization, predictive distribution | Conjugate computation in `R` | Problem set 2 |
| 4 | Conjugate models and marginal likelihoods | Beta-Binomial, Gamma-Poisson, Normal-Normal | Posterior predictive simulation | Lab 1 |
| 5 | Decision theory and prior design | Posterior risk, Bayes estimators, Jeffreys priors | Prior predictive checking | Problem set 3 |
| 6 | Numerical integration and Monte Carlo | Laplace, importance sampling | Variance reduction in `R` | Lab 2 |
| 7 | MCMC foundations | Detailed balance, ergodicity, MH ratio | Implement MH and Gibbs | Problem set 4 |
| 8 | HMC, NUTS, reparameterization | Hamiltonian dynamics, leapfrog | Stan diagnostics | Lab 3 |
| 9 | Variational inference and SMC | ELBO, particle approximations | ADVI and sequential update demos | Short memo |
| 10 | Bayesian linear and generalized linear models | Posterior regression structure | `cmdstanr` regression workflow | Problem set 5 |
| 11 | Hierarchical models and mixtures | Partial pooling, label switching | Non-centered parameterization | Lab 4 |
| 12 | Time series, nonparametrics, spatial and causal models | Filtering, GP covariance, DP intuition | Specialized model workflow | Reading response |
| 13 | Optimization, tuning, and diagnostics | MAP, SBC, PPC, LOO | R-hat, ESS, divergences, E-BFMI | Problem set 6 |
| 14 | R, Stan, JAX workflow integration; advanced topics and projects | Model averaging, robustness, communication | Reproducible final pipeline | Final project |

## Recommended Reading Spine

The course can be taught with a primary and secondary reading strategy.

### Primary references

- @gelman2013 for practical Bayesian workflow and multilevel modeling.
- @hoff2009 for a clean mathematical introduction.
- @bernardo1994 for formal foundations and decision theory.
- @robert2004 and @brooks2011 for computational methods.

### Secondary references

- @mcelreath2020 for intuition and pedagogy.
- @betancourt2017 for HMC geometry.
- @blei2017 and @kucukelbir2017 for variational inference.
- @rasmussen2006 for Gaussian processes.
- @murphy2023 for modern probabilistic machine learning connections.

## Self-Study Sequence

The self-study track is slower and more deliberate. A reasonable pacing plan is:

| Block | Weeks | Chapters | Goal |
|---|---:|---|---|
| A | 1-5 | 1-3 | Solidify probability, likelihood, and posterior logic |
| B | 6-10 | 4-6 | Become fluent in conjugacy, loss, and priors |
| C | 11-18 | 7-12 | Build computational maturity |
| D | 19-24 | 13-20 | Work through model families |
| E | 25-28 | 21-31 | Diagnostics, workflow, advanced topics, project |

For self-study, the minimum viable sequence is:

1. Read the theory chapter or part overview.
2. Reproduce one worked derivation on paper.
3. Run or retype one code example.
4. Complete two exercises: one analytic and one computational.
5. Write a one-paragraph reflection on diagnostics or modeling assumptions.

## Learning Outcomes By Assessment Type

### Derivation sets

Students should prove, derive, or verify:

- conjugate posterior forms;
- marginal likelihood calculations;
- the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio;
- HMC equations and the leapfrog map;
- the ELBO decomposition;
- Kalman filtering recursions in linear Gaussian state-space models.

### Computational labs

Students should implement:

- conjugate updating and predictive simulation;
- importance sampling and ESS calculations;
- random-walk Metropolis and Gibbs samplers;
- Stan models with posterior predictive checks;
- JAX gradient and Hessian calculations for MAP and Laplace approximations.

### Project work

Students should demonstrate:

- principled prior design;
- justified model structure;
- convergence diagnostics;
- posterior and prior predictive checks;
- sensitivity analysis;
- clearly written uncertainty communication.

## Prerequisites Checklist

Use the following checklist before starting the full course.

| Skill | Required level | Where to review if weak |
|---|---|---|
| Single-variable calculus | Comfortable | Appendix and standard calculus text |
| Multivariable differentiation | Comfortable | Optimization chapters and JAX chapter |
| Linear algebra | Comfortable | Regression and HMC chapters |
| Probability | Intermediate | [Measure-probability review](appendix/measure-probability-review.qmd) |
| Programming | Basic `R` or Python | [Software setup](appendix/software-setup.qmd) |

## Suggested Weekly Rhythm

For an instructor-led course, each week should ideally include:

1. One lecture focused on mathematical structure and derivations.
2. One lecture or lab focused on computation and diagnostics.
3. A small problem set mixing analytic derivations, simulation, and interpretation.

## What To Emphasize Pedagogically

Across the full syllabus, several misconceptions recur. The course should repeatedly correct them.

- A posterior distribution is not a likelihood with axes relabeled.
- A credible interval and a confidence interval solve different inferential problems.
- Good convergence diagnostics do not certify a good model.
- Fast optimization is not a substitute for posterior uncertainty quantification.
- Posterior predictive fit does not imply causal validity.
- Bayes factors are highly prior-sensitive and should not be used reflexively.

## Suggested Milestones

By the midpoint of the course, students should already be able to:

- derive and implement at least three conjugate models;
- explain and code a random-walk Metropolis sampler;
- interpret acceptance rates, autocorrelation, ESS, and $\hat{R}$;
- run a simple Stan model and diagnose obvious pathologies.

By the end of the course, students should be able to:

- fit and criticize multilevel and generalized linear Bayesian models;
- explain why centered and non-centered parameterizations behave differently;
- use predictive criteria and posterior checks to compare models;
- integrate `R`, `Stan`, and `JAX` into a coherent reproducible workflow.
